coredata.dc:title: Quantification measurement of hemoglobin with large dynamic range and low detection limit via an optical fiber optofluidic laser with enzyme-catalyzed reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hongrui
Zhang, Ya-nan
Wang, Mingyue
Zhao, Yong
Han, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Quantitative measurement of hemoglobin (Hb) levels is an essential part of routine medical examinations, disease diagnosis and health status monitoring. In this study, we introduced optical fiber optofluidic laser (FOFL) technology combined with catalytic oxidation reaction to design a laser sensor for sensitive Hb detection. For the H2O2-rhodamine B (RhB) oxidation system, radially emitting FOFL was achieved with a thin-walled hollow optical fiber (HOF) as an optical microcavity and amplified the concentration change of RhB during oxidation by H2O2. Hb was employed as peroxide-mimicking enzyme to catalyze the oxidation system, which sped up the reaction, resulting in an earlier laser extinction time. With the laser extinction time as sensing signal, the Hb sensor achieved a dynamic range of five orders of magnitude and a limit of detection (LOD) of 46.0 pM at an assay time of 40 min. The developed method provides ideas for the exploitation of FOFL biosensing based on catalytic oxidation reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical fiber optofluidic laser
Biochemical sensor
Hemoglobin detection
Enzyme-catalyzed reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of experimental apparatus for the FOFL sensor, with insets showing the changes in the FOFL spectra of organic dye oxidized by H2O2 and the effect of Hb on the oxidation reaction, respectively. The upper right inset shows microscopic image of the fabricated thin-walled HOF.

Effect of RhB concentration on laser output results. FOFL spectra at different RhB concentrations in mixed solutions (a) without and (b) with H2O2. (c) RhB concentration versus spectrally integrated intensity.
Effect of H2O2 concentration on laser output results. (a) FOFL spectra at different H2O2 concentrations in mixed solutions. (b) H2O2 concentration versus difference in integrated intensity.
(a) FOFL spectra at different pump energy densities. (b) Pump energy density versus spectrally integrated intensity, showing a laser threshold of about 20 μJ/mm2.

Emission spectra of FOFL with time (a) in the absence and (b) presence of 5 μM Hb. (c) Spectrally integrated intensity versus reaction time at different Hb concentrations. The spectral integration range was from 535 nm to 640 nm. The laser extinction time at each concentration is marked by the same color circle. (d) The relationship between the reciprocal of laser extinction time,
1 / t
, and Hb concentration. Error bars were calculated from three repeated measurements. (e) Relationship between spectrally integrated intensity and reaction time from three mixtures. The inset shows a comparison of their laser extinction times. Error bars were calculated from three repeated measurements.
Comparison of different methods for hemoglobin detection.

head.ce:title: Quantification measurement of hemoglobin with large dynamic range and low detection limit via an optical fiber optofluidic laser with enzyme-catalyzed reaction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries, Northeastern University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Precision Optical Sensing and Measurement Technology, Qinhuangdao 066004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Precision Optical Sensing and Measurement Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hebei Key Laboratory of Micro-Nano Precision Optical Sensing and Measurement Technology, Qinhuangdao, 066004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantitative measurement of hemoglobin (Hb) levels is an essential part of routine medical examinations, disease diagnosis and health status monitoring. In this study, we introduced optical fiber optofluidic laser (FOFL) technology combined with catalytic oxidation reaction to design a laser sensor for sensitive Hb detection. For the H2O2-rhodamine B (RhB) oxidation system, radially emitting FOFL was achieved with a thin-walled hollow optical fiber (HOF) as an optical microcavity and amplified the concentration change of RhB during oxidation by H2O2. Hb was employed as peroxide-mimicking enzyme to catalyze the oxidation system, which sped up the reaction, resulting in an earlier laser extinction time. With the laser extinction time as sensing signal, the Hb sensor achieved a dynamic range of five orders of magnitude and a limit of detection (LOD) of 46.0 pM at an assay time of 40 min. The developed method provides ideas for the exploitation of FOFL biosensing based on catalytic oxidation reactions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemoglobin (Hb), a special protein in red blood cells, functions in transporting oxygen and carbon dioxide as well as maintaining the acid-base balance of the blood [1]. The decrease and increase of Hb level in human blood have clinical significance, its increase may be related to congenital cardiopulmonary diseases, tumor or kidney diseases, and the decrease may be related to various types of anemia or the decline of bone marrow hematopoietic function [2–4]. Therefore, the determination of Hb content in human serum is an essential part of clinical testing. In addition, for the determination of the level of a substance in blood, reasonable dilution of the sample can reduce the interference of other substances in the serum and greatly reduce the sample consumption [5]. At the same time, the detection of low concentration Hb is necessary for the diagnosis and treatment of some diseases, such as intravascular hemolytic diseases. The measurement of trace free Hb in plasma is quite important for evaluating the degree of hemolysis and judging the therapeutic effect [6]. Therefore, it is meaningful to design Hb sensor with low LOD. Optical methods are one of the mainstream techniques for protein detection, such as fluorescence [7–13], colorimetry [14], and spectrophotometry [15], etc. In 2014, Pang et al. designed an Hb sensor that uses trypsin to cleave Hb and release heme, which binds to oligonucleotide sequence to form a complex that leads to fluorescence quenching, with a linear range of 5–200 nM and a LOD of 2 nM [11]. At the same year, Wang et al. proposed a method for Hb determination utilizing fluorescence quenching of synthesized CdHgSe quantum dots with a linear range of 4.0×10−9-4.4×10−7 M and a LOD of 2.0×10−9 M [10]. However, a certain amount of time consumption, complex and cumbersome operating procedures, sample volume requirements, or possibly high LOD to some extent limit the potential for further development of light-based Hb sensors. Therefore, it is necessary to continue to develop a fast, sensitive, convenient and micro-scale Hb detection method.

Optofluidic laser (OFL) technology combines photonics and microfluidics, and is a novel platform technology for studying the interaction between light and biochemical substances [16–20]. Compared with conventional fluorescence, optical microcavities are added to OFL to provide optical feedback, and spontaneous radiation is transformed into stimulated radiation, which gives the laser output signal directionality, narrow linewidth and nonlinear characteristics not found in fluorescence, which are beneficial for biochemical sensing performance. Due to the enhancement of light-matter interaction, OFL is able to sensitively reflect changes in the state of biomolecules in the gain medium. Therefore, OFL has the potential to achieve higher biochemical sensing sensitivity than conventional biochemical sensing means [21–28]. In addition, hollow optical fibers (HOFs) play a great role in the design of biochemical sensors using optical fiber optofluidic laser (FOFL) benefit from their unique circular hollow structure that can be served as optical microcavities while integrating natural microfluidic channels [29–33]. The use of HOF as a laser cavity for FOFL is especially beneficial for achieving fast, convenient and micro-scale biochemical sensing [31,32]. In 2020, Yang et al. reported a scheme for large-scale production of thin-walled HOFs and demonstrated disposable immune-sensing based on HOF-OFL with a LOD of 11 nM, an assay time of 15 min, and a sample volume of 735 nL [31]. In 2021, Yang et al. continued to use HOF to propose a sequentially bio-conjugated scheme to address the bioassay washing problem of microstructured optical fibers, and obtained a dynamic range of 4 orders of magnitude in biomolecular assay with a LOD of 9.5 pM, an assay time of 25 min and a reagent volume of 589 nL [32].

In view of the advantages that FOFL can bring to biochemical sensing, we designed an FOFL for Hb detection. The peroxidase catalytic property of Hb was used to catalyze the oxidative degradation of RhB by H2O2, and an FOFL detection platform was constructed to achieve the measurement of Hb concentration. The effect of different Hb concentrations on the reaction rate of the H2O2-RhB oxidation reaction system was explored, which was reflected in the difference of reaction time when FOFL was just extinguished. By employing the laser extinction time as the sensing signal, the designed Hb sensor achieved a dynamic range of 5 orders of magnitude with a LOD of 46.0 pM at an assay time of 40 min. FOFL-based biochemical sensors brought some advantages to Hb detection, including short detection time, low sample consumption, simple and convenient operation, as well as improved LOD, and also provide a new idea for combining catalytic oxidation reactions with FOFL to achieve high-performance biochemical sensing.

Rhodamine B (RhB), an oxanthene organic dye with the molecular structure shown in the inset of Fig. 1, is often used as gain medium in OFL [34]. Under the action of the appropriate pump light, the population inversion can be realized, and the gain of the whole laser system can be provided by the stimulated radiation. In the environment of strong alkaline solution, RhB can be oxidatively degraded by H2O2, resulting in a reduction in the number of dye molecules in solution that can participate in luminescence, which in turn leads to fluorescence quenching [35–37]. In addition, Hb, as a well-known iron metalloprotein, has peroxidase catalytic activity. The reaction between iron-rich Hb and H2O2 acts as an important factor in the enhancement of fluorescence quenching. In 1984, Sadrzadeh et al. reported that Hb can be used as a biological Fenton catalyst [38]. The molar reaction of excess H2O2 with Hb can result in the degradation of heme and the release of iron, which can promote the hydroxyl free radical formation in Fenton reaction. As a strong oxidizing agent, hydroxyl free radicals can increase the fluorescence quenching of organic dyes to a certain extent [39,40].
(1)
Fe
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
→
Fe
3 +
+
HO
∙
+
OH
−

Combined with the catalytic kinetic analysis method, Hb, as a peroxide-mimicking enzyme, can catalyze the oxidation system of H2O2-RhB, resulting in an accelerated rate of RhB discoloration reaction. According to the rate equation for the catalytic discoloration reaction, there is a negligible change in the concentration of H2O2 when there is a large excess of H2O2 in solution and a large change in the concentration of RhB in the reaction [39]. Therefore, the basic relationship equation for the catalytic discoloration reaction with Hb as the catalyst is obtained as follows
(2)
ln
C
0
C
R
=
K
′
C
H
t
where t is the reaction time,
C
R
is the concentration of RhB in solution,
C
0
is the initial concentration of RhB at reaction time
t = 0
,
K
′
is a constant, and
C
H
is the concentration of Hb as catalyst for the oxidation reaction. The fixed concentration method was used for quantitative analysis of Hb. By fixing
C
R
as a constant value, the concentration of Hb is inversely proportional to the time t at which the concentration of RhB reaches
C
R
in the oxidation reaction, and is linear with the reciprocal of time t .

Based on the above, a Hb sensor can be constructed using the FOFL detection system. As shown in the inset of Fig. 1, the optical amplification effect in the laser generation process enables the FOFL to sensitively detect small variations in RhB molecule numbers, which are represented by changes in the intensity of the dye laser. The fixed concentration method was used to quantitatively analyze Hb. The laser threshold concentration
C
R
′
, that is, the critical dye concentration required for laser emission generation, was chosen as the dye fixation concentration achieved in the catalytic discoloration reaction. When the dye concentration in the solution is greater than
C
R
′
, the laser appears at a certain intensity, and when the dye concentration is less than
C
R
′
, the laser disappears without laser intensity. By using the time t for the dye concentration to be reduced to the laser threshold concentration
C
R
′
(i.e. the time it takes for the laser to go from being present to just completely extinguished), defined here as the laser extinction time, as the sensing signal, the relationship between Hb concentration and laser extinction time can be established.

Fig. 1 illustrates a schematic of the experimental setup for the FOFL sensor. Both the microfluidic channel and the optical microcavity were provided by a thin-walled HOF (wall thickness about 4 μm, inner diameter 250 μm), whose microscopic image is shown in the upper right inset of Fig. 1. Thin-walled HOF was fabricated by using the hydrofluoric acid etching, and details of the fabrication process can be found in reference [33]. It has a high quality factor (Q-factor) due to the thin wall thickness, and its cross-section provides a ring resonator supporting whispering gallery modes (WGMs) [33]. The WGMs constantly interact with the liquid gain medium flowing inside the HOF through evanescent waves, thus supplying optical feedback for the generation of FOFL.

The flow of liquid in the HOF was achieved through a peristaltic pump connected to a soft tube, the other end of which was connected to the HOF and secured with ultraviolet adhesive. A laser beam from a 532 nm pulsed laser (Continuum, Minilite II) was concentrated through a lens onto the thin-walled HOF to excite the optofluidic WGM laser. The regulation of the pumping energy was achieved by an adjustable neutral density filter and monitored in real time by an energy meter (Ophir, PE10-C). Both were used to keep the pump energy of the laser fixed near the desired value during the experiment. The output laser was captured by another lens and sent by a multimode fiber to a computer-connected spectrometer (Ocean Optics, Flame). The spectral data were observed and recorded by OceanView software, and the next data processing was carried out by Origin software.

In order to reduce the interference of external light, all measurement experiments were carried out in a dark room. During the preparation and conduct of the measurement experiment and the analysis of the results, the consistency of experimental conditions and experimental operations was ensured as much as possible, and the same data processing was carried out on the experimental results. This ensured the stability of the measurement system to a certain extent and brought help to the reproducibility of the measurement results. During the measurement, the output spectra can be regarded as a real-time reflection of changes in the amount of the gain medium in the liquid sample flowing in the HOF. Since OFL system is sensitive to changes in the gain medium, which is reflected in the increase or decrease of laser intensity, the progress of the reaction in the solution can be monitored by observing changes in the laser spectra.

The organic dye rhodamine B (RhB, Aladdin, R104961) was used as laser gain medium for FOFL and diluted with ultrapure water to obtain 5 mM RhB stock solution. H2O2 solution (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 30 %) was diluted with ultrapure water to prepare 0.3 % H2O2 solution (prepared before use). Hemoglobin (Hb, Macklin, H828504) was diluted with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, HyClone, SH30256) to prepare a 200 μM Hb stock solution. NH3-NH4Cl buffer solution with pH of 10.0 was prepared by weighing 1 g sodium chloride (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., 10019318) and dissolving it in 45 mL ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 5 mL stronger ammonia water (Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.). Ultrapure water was obtained from the laboratory water purification system.

In the experiments, H2O2 solution, Hb solution, RhB solution, and NH3-NH4Cl buffer were homogeneously mixed at a certain volume ratio. Then, the mixture was pumped into the thin-walled HOF with a peristaltic pump and a fixed flow rate was maintained to avoid the photobleaching effect of the organic dye molecules caused by the continuous action of the pump laser as much as possible. During the experiment, the sample solution was excited by 532 nm pump light, and the laser emission spectra were recorded in real time. At the end of each test, the mixed solution was pumped out of the thin-walled HOF. The interior of the HOF was rinsed with ethanol for 3 minutes to remove the residual sample solution, and then rinsed three times with ultrapure water.

The RhB stock solution was diluted to a range of different concentrations (0.1 mM-4.8 mM) with ultrapure water to study the effects of RhB concentration on the oxidation reaction. At the same time, the 0.3 % H2O2 solution was diluted to 0.03 % with ultrapure water. In order to compare the oxidative degradation of RhB by H2O2, two groups of mixed solutions were prepared simultaneously. In the first group, RhB solution with different concentrations, NH3-NH4Cl buffer, and ultrapure water were homogeneously mixed in a volume ratio of 1:1:2. In the second group, 0.03 % H2O2 solution, different concentrations of RhB solution, NH3-NH4Cl buffer, and ultrapure water were homogeneously mixed in the same volume ratio. The two sets of mixtures were left for 2 hours at room temperature to ensure the complete reaction, after which they were passed into the HOF in increasing order of RhB concentration, and excited by pump light with pump energy of 53.36 μJ/mm2 to obtain laser emission.

Fig. 2(a) and (b) show the output spectra from two sets of mixed solutions with different RhB concentrations, respectively. The laser peaks near 532 nm were unfiltered pump light emitted by the pulsed laser. Fig. 2(c) shows the relationship between RhB concentration and the spectrally integrated intensity. As can be seen, at lower RhB concentrations, the concentration of gain medium was not sufficient to support FOFL emission, and there was no significant change in the spectrally integrated intensity. As RhB increased to a certain concentration, FOFL began to appear and the laser intensity went on increasing with RhB concentration. At the same time, when H2O2 was present in the mixed solution, the minimum RhB concentration supporting laser emission was significantly greater than that without H2O2. Meanwhile, the spectrally integrated intensity with H2O2 was significantly lower than that without H2O2 at the same RhB concentration after laser emergence (below 1 mM). The RhB concentration that reached self-quenching was also higher than that without H2O2. These results indicate that RhB can be degraded by H2O2. Based on the above experimental results, 0.5 mM was chosen as the fixed concentration of RhB in the mixed solution for subsequent experiments. The reason was that the concentration was large enough to support stronger laser emission, while small enough not to cause significant self-quenching. At the same time, dye laser intensity near 0.5 mM concentration was greatly affected by changes in RhB concentration, which allowed FOFL to reflect small changes in RhB concentration, thus providing more room for the oxidation effect of H2O2.

To test the effect of H2O2 concentration on the laser output results, the H2O2 solution with concentration of 0.3 % was diluted to obtain a series of H2O2 dilutions with concentrations between 0.003 % and 0.042 %. H2O2 solution with different concentrations, RhB solution with 2 mM concentration, NH3-NH4Cl buffer, and ultrapure water were mixed homogeneously at the same volume ratio. After that, the mixed solution was left at room temperature for 2 hours to ensure that the reaction was completed thoroughly. Meanwhile, a mixed solution with ultrapure water instead of H2O2 was prepared as a blank solution. The mixed solution was passed into HOF, which was excited by pump light with pump energy density of 40.90 μJ/mm2.

Fig. 3(a) shows the output spectra from mixed solutions with different H2O2 concentrations, and Fig. 3(b) presents the relationship between the H2O2 concentration and the difference in integrated intensity
∆ I =
I
0
− I
, in which I was the spectrally integrated intensity of the mixed solution containing H2O2 and
I
0
was that of the blank solution. Fig. 3(b) shows that the difference in integrated intensity was linearly enhanced with increasing H2O2 concentration at the range of 0.00075 %-0.0075 %. In the range of 0.0075 %-0.0105 % H2O2 concentration the laser emission was no longer generated, which was due to the fact that the larger concentration of H2O2 in the mixed solution caused the organic dye RhB to be sufficiently oxidized, and its concentration was reduced below the threshold concentration, i.e. the critical concentration at which the dye solution can be excited to produce laser emission. At higher H2O2 concentrations, the dye was more strongly oxidized and no laser was produced.

For the selection of H2O2 concentration, two factors need to be considered. On the one hand, the concentration of H2O2 should be large enough to allow the organic dye RhB in the mixed solution to be sufficiently oxidized and its concentration reduced to below the threshold concentration to ensure that the laser is completely extinguished, which is beneficial for the detection of lower concentrations of peroxide-mimicking enzyme. At the same time, according to the rate equation for the catalytic discoloration reaction, the basic relationship Eq. (2) can be satisfied only when H2O2 in the solution is excessive. On the other hand, the presence of a particularly high concentration of H2O2 in the mixed solution would lead to a faster oxidation reaction rate, making the laser extinction time shorter, which would ultimately lead to indistinguishable laser extinction times at different peroxide-mimicking enzyme concentrations to be measured. Based on the trade-off between these two factors, a fixed H2O2 concentration of 0.75 % was chosen for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of optical fiber optofluidic laser
ce:section.ce:para: The FOFL was characterized employing thin-walled HOF as WGM optical microcavity, 532 nm pulsed laser as pump source and 0.5 mM RhB in NH3-NH4Cl buffer solution as gain medium. The output spectra at different pump energy densities were obtained as shown in Fig. 4(a). From the results in the figure, it can be seen that the laser emission peaks appear gradually with the increase of the pump energy density and enhance rapidly after their appearance. At pumping energy density of 36.36 μJ/mm2, the full width at half maximum of the obtained FOFL (<4.49 nm) was significantly narrower than that of fluorescence. Fig. 4(b) shows the spectrally integrated intensity versus pump energy density, demonstrating the nonlinear characteristics of the laser output process and indicating a laser threshold of about 20 μJ/mm2. A lower laser threshold is advantageous to the sensitivity of the designed biochemical sensor.

In FOFL detection systems, the optical amplification in the laser generation process enables the laser to sensitively detect small variations in the amount of RhB and realize signal amplification. The variation of the amount of RhB in the solution can be reflected by the variation of the obtained FOFL intensity. According to the basic relationship Eq. (2) of the catalytic discoloration reaction, the time t required for RhB concentration to decrease to the laser threshold concentration
C
R
′
was defined here as the laser extinction time. The reciprocal of the laser extinction time,
1 / t
, was closely related to the Hb concentration, which was employed as the sensing signal to reflect the Hb concentration change. The final value of the laser intensity reduction was determined by the concentration of H2O2. Here, the chosen H2O2 concentration can completely extinguish the dye laser intensity at 0.5 mM RhB concentration.

The Hb stock solution was serially tenfold diluted with PBS to prepare a set of concentrations within 200 pM to 20 μM. Next, H2O2 solution, different concentrations of Hb solution, RhB solution, and NH3-NH4Cl buffer were mixed homogeneously at the same volume ratio to obtain a total volume of 600 μL of mixed solution. At the same time, a mixed solution without Hb was prepared as a blank solution. Immediately after the preparation of each mixed solution, the mixture was pumped into the thin-walled HOF and kept flowing at a flow rate of 10 μL/min, immediately excited with pump light. The output spectra at different reaction times were recorded until the laser completely disappeared and remained stable for a period of time. It is worth noting that the small size of the thin-walled HOF (inner diameter 250 μm), as well as the low flow rate of the liquid during the measurement, helped to control the sample consumption to some extent. Therefore, the total volume of the prepared mixture of 600 μL was more than sufficient for the actual amount of the sample required for the assay.

Fig. 5(a) and (b) show the laser emission spectra for different reaction times in the absence and presence of Hb in the mixed solution, respectively. The pump energy density was set to 68.56 μJ/mm2. As the oxidation reaction proceeded, the organic dye RhB was gradually oxidized and decolored, and its concentration decreased. As a result, the intensity of the laser emission gradually decreased, disappeared completely after a certain period of time, and did not change thereafter. Hb, as a peroxide-mimicking enzyme, can accelerate the reaction rate of the H2O2-RhB oxidation system. In the absence of Hb in the mixture, the laser emission disappeared completely at 35 min. In the presence of Hb in the mixture, the laser emission disappeared completely at 18 min, with a significantly shorter extinction time.

The spectrally integrated intensity versus reaction time at different Hb concentrations is shown in Fig. 5(c). The oxidation reaction rate as a whole showed an overall trend of rapidity followed by slowness. At the beginning of the reaction, a sufficient concentration of H2O2 was contained in the solution to make the oxidation reaction rate faster, and the integrated intensity of the laser showed a steady downward trend. With the continuation of the reaction, the oxidation reaction rate slowed down significantly when the dye concentration was reduced to near the laser threshold concentration
C
R
′
, and the rate of integrated intensity reduction was decreased. The reason for this situation was that the catalytic reaction was gradually becoming saturated at this time, and the laser intensity was gradually reaching a relatively stable state. Eventually, the laser intensity gradually decreased to the minimum value over time (laser extinction), and the laser extinction time decreased with increasing Hb concentration of the solution. The reciprocal of the laser extinction time,
1 / t
, was used as the sensing signal. Fig. 5(d) shows the functions relationship between
1 / t
and Hb concentration on a logarithmic scale. It can be seen that there was a positively correlated between
1 / t
and the Hb concentration, which reflected the acceleration of the oxidation reaction rate in terms of the kinetics of the enzyme-catalyzed reaction. After linear fitting of the experimental data points, the reciprocal of the laser extinction time,
1 / t
, increased linearly with increasing Hb concentration in the 50 pM to 5 μM range. From the experimental results, the designed Hb sensor achieved a dynamic range of five orders of magnitude, and the LOD was 46.0 pM, which was derived from the calculation of
3 σ / k
(“ k ” is the slope of the linearly fitted curve, and “ σ ” is the standard deviation of the blank measurements) [32].

Several substances present in blood were selected as interfering substances, including glucose, urea, bovine serum albumin, sodium ions and chloride ions, which were added to the Hb solution and experimentally measured the effect of foreign substances on the sensor. Three groups of mixtures were prepared, one without Hb as a control, one with Hb, and one with the aforementioned interfering substances as a further addition. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 5(e). Shorter laser extinction time was obtained in the presence of Hb, while the presence of interfering substances lengthened it slightly. However, compared with the control group, the laser extinction time still showed a substantial reduction.

Compared with traditional fluorescence sensing, the presence of optical microcavities in OFL enhanced the interaction between light and matter, giving OFL ability to respond to subtle changes in biomolecules inside the microcavity, which is advantageous for achieving lower LOD. The use of thin-walled HOF on the one hand provided a high Q-factor to support the excitation of FOFL with lower laser threshold, and on the other hand, its hollow structure feature also provided convenience for the replacement of sample solution and the washing of the interior of the channel. At the same time, the homogeneous detection method reduced the surface molecule fixation time required by traditional non-homogeneous detection methods, which is advantageous to achieve rapid protein detection. In our assay experiment, the time required for each protein concentration was 40 min. In contrast, fluorescence takes longer to extinguish and is harder to observe accurately. In addition, it is worth noting that the inherent small size of HOF allows for only a small sample volume to be required in assay experiments, which reduces the sample consumption to a relatively large extent. Table I demonstrates the performance comparison of the sensor proposed in this paper with other commonly used Hb sensors, showing the competitiveness in terms of LOD and dynamic range.

Several strategies to further improve protein detection using FOFL are discussed here. The laser output mainly comes from multiple round trips of oscillations, which are highly correlated with the interaction of light and matter, suggesting that high Q-factors are crucial for optical microcavities that provide optical feedback to laser systems. By improving the optical microcavity, it is expected to obtain a laser cavity with a higher Q factor to further greatly enhance the light-matter interaction, which is conducive to achieving a lower concentration of Hb detection, thereby reducing the LOD of the sensor. In addition, a reasonable increase in the energy density value of the pump light may also be advantageous for large dynamic range detection.

In conclusion, we proposed and demonstrated an FOFL biochemical sensor for Hb detection. In the H2O2-RhB oxidation system, the peroxidase catalytic properties of Hb were used to catalyze the oxidation of RhB and make it degrade. Using the FOFL detection platform, the variation of RhB molecular concentration caused by H2O2 can be amplified, which was reflected in the variation of laser intensity. It can be used to further distinguish the rate of oxidation reaction induced by different concentrations of Hb, which was reflected in the difference in reaction times when the FOFL was exactly extinguished. On this basis, the proposed FOFL sensor was used to realize Hb sensing achieving five orders of magnitude dynamic range and 46.0 pM LOD at an assay time of 40 min. As a rapid, homogeneous, low sample consumption, simple process and low-cost biochemical analysis method, FOFL has a potential for the development of light-based Hb sensors, which can provide a new direction for the diagnosis of related diseases.

Yong Zhao: Project administration. Bo Han: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Ya-nan Zhang: Supervision, Investigation. Hongrui Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Mingyue Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Key R&D Program of China under grant 2022YFB3808800, National Natural Science Foundation of China under grants 62222504, U22A2021, and 62375045, LiaoNing Revitalization Talents Program under grant XLYC2203125, State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries under grant 2018ZCX31, and Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province under grant F2024501044.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bo Han received her M.A. degrees and Ph.D. degrees, respectively, in 2013 and 2021 from the College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, China. Now he is working in Northeastern University as an associate professor. His research interests include optical fiber sensors and flow rate sensors. He has authored and co-authored more than 20 scientific papers and conference presentations.
